case_name: Accident Compensation Corporation v AZ [2023] NZCA 617; [2023] 3 NZLR 445 (6 December 2023)

summary.brief: Accident Compensation Corporation v AZ [2023] NZCA 617; [2023] 3 NZLR 445 (6 December 2023). Medical malpractice case involving obstetrics gynaecology.

evidence_index.text: AZ, who is now 20 years old, developed spina bifida in utero at approximately five weeks’ gestation.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: AZ’s mother underwent a 20-week ultrasound scan when she was almost 20 weeks pregnant. The spina bifida should have been detected by the health professional who read the scan.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: It is an accepted fact that had AZ’s mother been informed the foetus had spina bifida, she would have sought and obtained a lawful termination of her pregnancy when she was about 20 weeks pregnant.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: We are satisfied that the terms “treatment” and “treatment injury” in ss 32 and 33 of the Act are broader than the traditional curative-focused definitions of treatment relied upon by ACC.
evidence_index.speaker: Court reasoning

evidence_index.text: If AZ has personal injuries that are a treatment injury, she has cover under the Act.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: AZ was born with spina bifida and several other related health conditions.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The spinal defects associated with spina bifida satisfy the natural and ordinary meaning of the term physical injury.
evidence_index.speaker: Court reasoning

evidence_index.text: To hold that there was no cover in the circumstances of this case would be to create what the Court of Appeal majority in Accident Compensation Corporation v D recognised would be an “odd” gap in the general coverage for medical misadventure.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: The exclusion is not for natural processes per se. It is for gradual processes that are not of a certain kind. The focus is on the cause of the gradual process.
evidence_index.speaker: Court reasoning

initial_state.chief_complaint: Not applicable as the case involves prenatal diagnosis
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: AZ developed spina bifida in utero at approximately five weeks’ gestation. The condition was not detected during a 20-week ultrasound scan.

focused_exam.system: Examination mentioned but findings not specified

initial_state.initial_working_problem: Undiagnosed spina bifida during prenatal period

requestables.description: Ultrasound scan performed at 20 weeks of gestation

reveal.detailed_findings: Imaging performed but findings not detailed in judgment
reveal.decision_impact: Failure to detect led to missed opportunity for termination

decision_points.prompt: What should have been done upon reviewing the ultrasound scan?

options.description: Correctly identify spina bifida on the ultrasound
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard practice to identify major fetal abnormalities

expected_action_court.description: Spina bifida should have been detected
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence established detection is standard

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to detect spina bifida led to missed opportunity for termination
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Spina bifida should have been identified, allowing for informed decision-making

patient_outcome.description: AZ was born with spina bifida due to missed diagnosis at 20-week scan.
patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Chronic health issues related to spina bifida

key_findings: Failure to diagnose spina bifida at 20-week scan
Causation established - missed opportunity for termination

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to diagnose spina bifida at 20-week scan
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: If diagnosed, termination could have been considered

Failure to diagnose spina bifida at 20-week scan

court_findings.finding: Defendant breached duty of care by failing to diagnose spina bifida
